figures.gloss: The absolutely simple, ineffable, and transcendent source of all reality. It does not act, desire, or create through will; rather, reality overflows from its infinite perfection like light radiating from the sun, undiminished by its continuous giving.
figures.facet: It is utterly devoid of attributes—even 'being' or 'mind' are considered too limiting to describe it. It is not a creator god who chooses to make a universe, but the gravitational center of all existence that generates reality by sheer superabundance.
figures.provenance: Plotinus, 'Enneads' (VI.9); Porphyry; Proclus.

figures.gloss: The first emanation of the One, a timeless realm of perfect, intuitive thought. It holds the Platonic Forms—the eternal, living templates of everything that exists. It is the locus where the One first gazes back upon itself, creating the duality of thinker and thought.
figures.facet: Unlike human discursive reason, which moves from premise to conclusion, Nous perceives all truth simultaneously in an eternal present. It is not a geographic place, but the unified totality of all true being and living intellect.
figures.provenance: Plotinus, 'Enneads' (V.1, V.9).

figures.gloss: The second emanation, proceeding from Nous to animate the material cosmos. Psyche bridges the eternal and the temporal, translating the static, perfect Forms of the Intellect into the unfolding, moving reality of time, space, and living beings.
figures.facet: She faces two directions simultaneously: gazing upward to contemplate the perfect Intellect, and looking downward to weave its patterns into the dark, formless expanse of Matter, generating the physical universe without being corrupted by it.
figures.provenance: Plotinus, 'Enneads' (IV.8); Proclus, 'Elements of Theology'.

figures.gloss: The absolute bottom of the emanative chain, where the light of the One finally fades into nothing. Matter in its pure state is sheer non-being, a formless substrate that receives the reflections of the Soul but possesses no reality of its own.
figures.facet: It explains the existence of evil without conceding a dualistic rival to the Good. Evil is not an active, malevolent force, but a mere shadow—the absolute exhaustion of emanation, a sheer privation where the light of being has failed to reach.
figures.provenance: Plotinus, 'Enneads' (I.8).

figures.gloss: Literally 'god-working', a system of late Neoplatonic ritual practice using material objects, incantations, and symbols to align the practitioner's soul with the divine hierarchy. It forces a descent of the divine presence, elevating the soul to mystical union.
figures.facet: It marks a violent break from Plotinus's purely intellectual mysticism. Iamblichus argued that human reason is too corrupted to ascend alone; salvation requires the mechanical, ritual use of divine tokens planted in the material world by the gods themselves.
figures.provenance: Iamblichus, 'De Mysteriis Aegyptiorum'; Proclus; Julian.

figures.gloss: The hidden signatures or energetic sympathies embedded within physical matter—specific stones, herbs, animals, and sounds—placed there by the Demiurge. They resonate invisibly with specific planetary or divine forces.
figures.facet: Because the divine generated all things, fragments of the divine mind are crystallized even in dirt and stones. Matter is therefore redeemed; these tokens wait to be activated by the theurgist as a physical ladder back to heaven.
figures.provenance: The 'Chaldean Oracles'; Iamblichus; Proclus, 'On the Sacred Art'.

figures.gloss: The fundamental cosmic motion whereby every emanated being yearns to return to its source. It is the upward, unifying thrust of the universe, compelling souls to shed material attachments and ascend back into the Intellect, and ultimately, the One.
figures.facet: Reality is not a static hierarchy, but a breathing circuit of 'proodos' (outpouring) and 'epistrophe' (return). The soul's salvation is simply fulfilling the cosmological law of gravity—falling upward back into its own ultimate origin.
figures.provenance: Proclus, 'Elements of Theology' (Props 31-35).

figures.gloss: In the Chaldean Oracles, Hekate is elevated from a dark underworld goddess to the cosmic World Soul herself. She stands at the boundary between the purely intellectual Father and the material realm, transmitting divine fire downward and leading human souls upward.
figures.facet: She strips away her terrifying, witchcraft-associated Greek guise to become a luminous, strictly metaphysical mediator. As the 'womb' of the cosmos, she actively shoots the 'Ideas' from the Father's mind down into physical generation.
figures.provenance: The 'Chaldean Oracles'; Psellus; Damascius.

figures.gloss: Egyptian-born philosopher (c. 204/5-270 CE) who studied under Ammonius Saccas in Alexandria and taught in Rome from 245 CE. His oral seminars and private treatises, later arranged by his student Porphyry into the six Enneads, laid out the emanation of Nous and Soul from the ineffable One and became the school's founding scripture.
figures.facet: He treated philosophy as a practice of ascent rather than a body of doctrine: Porphyry records that in their six years together Plotinus attained mystical union with the One four times, wrote without correcting a line, and refused to sit for a portrait.
figures.provenance: Porphyry, Life of Plotinus; Plotinus, Enneads.

figures.gloss: Tyrian disciple (c. 234-305 CE) who studied under Plotinus in Rome during the last six years of his teacher's life, arranged Plotinus's fifty-four treatises into six groups of nine (the Enneads), and wrote the biographical preface that is the main source for Plotinus's life.
figures.facet: He argued for intellectual ascent over ritual, challenging astrology and popular theurgy in his Letter to Anebo — the very skepticism Iamblichus's De Mysteriis exists to answer, splitting the school's method for a century.
figures.provenance: Porphyry, Life of Plotinus; Letter to Anebo (fragments preserved via Iamblichus and Eusebius).

figures.gloss: Syrian Neoplatonist (c. 245-325 CE), founder of the school's Syrian branch, whose De Mysteriis Aegyptiorum answered Porphyry's skepticism by grounding salvation in theurgy — ritual use of divine tokens planted in matter by the gods — rather than reason alone.
figures.facet: He inverted Plotinus's psychology: for Iamblichus the embodied soul cannot lift itself to the divine by thought alone, so the gods themselves must be induced to descend through consecrated symbols, sounds, and objects.
figures.provenance: Iamblichus, De Mysteriis Aegyptiorum.

figures.gloss: Head (diadochos) of the Athenian Academy in the fifth century CE, whose Elements of Theology cast the whole emanative hierarchy — remaining, procession, and reversion — as a chain of geometrically styled propositions.
figures.facet: He completed the systematization Iamblichus began, multiplying the intermediate ranks of gods, angels, daemons, and heroes into an exhaustively ordered ladder between the One and matter.
figures.provenance: Proclus, Elements of Theology; Platonic Theology.

figures.gloss: The craftsman-intellect of Plato's Timaeus, who shapes the visible cosmos after the eternal Forms. Neoplatonists identified him with Nous itself, or with a demiurgic power within it, and credited him with sowing the synthemata — divine tokens — into matter.
figures.facet: He is not a creator from nothing but an orderer of pre-existing chaos into likeness with the Forms — the point where the school's abstract emanation scheme meets Plato's own mythic cosmology.
figures.provenance: Plato, Timaeus; Plotinus, Enneads III.9; Proclus, Commentary on the Timaeus.

figures.gloss: The soul's final unification with the One, achieved beyond thought and being — not knowledge of the One but identity with it, however briefly held. Porphyry records Plotinus reaching this state four times during their six years together.
figures.facet: It is the terminus the entire emanative circuit exists to reach: not a reward added to contemplation but its dissolution, since even the highest thought still implies a duality of thinker and thought that henosis erases.
figures.provenance: Plotinus, Enneads VI.9; Porphyry, Life of Plotinus 23.

figures.gloss: The outward pouring of reality from each higher principle into the next, the generative down-stroke by which the One's superabundance yields Nous, Nous yields Soul, and Soul yields the visible cosmos.
figures.facet: Named as one half of a single circuit whose other half is epistrophe: nothing proceeds without eventually returning, so procession and reversion together, not creation alone, describe how the hierarchy holds itself in place.
figures.provenance: Proclus, Elements of Theology, props. 25-39.

figures.gloss: Fragmentary second-century CE hexameter verses, traditionally attributed to Julian the Chaldean and his son Julian the Theurgist, transmitted only in quotations by later Neoplatonists who treated them as direct divine revelation rather than philosophy.
figures.facet: Their authority runs opposite to Plotinus's method: where the Enneads argue, the Oracles simply pronounce — and it was this pronounced, ritual register that let Iamblichus and Proclus fold theurgy into a school built on reasoned argument.
figures.provenance: Chaldean Oracles (fragments ed. des Places); Psellus; Proclus; Damascius.

figures.gloss: The luminous 'vehicle' of the soul, acquired during its descent through the planetary spheres and progressively purified during its ascent back toward the One — the subtle body that makes theurgic ritual mechanically effective on an incarnate soul.
figures.facet: It solves a technical problem the philosophy otherwise leaves open: how a wholly immaterial soul can be acted on by physical rites at all — the ochema is the graduated interface where sacrament actually touches spirit.
figures.provenance: Proclus, Elements of Theology, props. 205-210; Synesius, De Insomniis.

figures.gloss: Intermediary spirits ranked in Iamblichus's hierarchy between gods, angels, heroes, and human souls, who administer the natural world and serve as the practical contact-points theurgic ritual is designed to engage.
figures.facet: Their classification turns the cosmos into a bureaucracy of descending rank rather than a flat pantheon — every theurgic act addresses a specific office in this chain, not an undifferentiated 'spirit world'.
figures.provenance: Iamblichus, De Mysteriis, Book II.

figures.gloss: Mathematician, astronomer, and head of the Alexandrian Neoplatonic school, teacher of Synesius of Cyrene (later a Christian bishop). She was killed by a Christian mob in March 415 CE, an event recorded by the church historian Socrates Scholasticus.
figures.facet: Her death marks the school's collision with an ascendant Christianity in the very city where its Alexandrian branch had flourished — philosophy's civic standing ending in a specific, datable act of violence rather than a slow doctrinal defeat.
figures.provenance: Socrates Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History VII.15; Damascius, Life of Isidore (fragments).

figures.gloss: Roman emperor (r. 361-363 CE), raised Christian but converted to Neoplatonist paganism under teachers in the Iamblichean tradition. His Hymn to King Helios (Oration IV) casts solar theurgy as the theological core of a briefly attempted imperial pagan restoration.
figures.facet: He is the one point where the school's private ritual metaphysics became state policy — theurgy was no longer a philosopher's discipline alone but, for two years, an emperor's program.
figures.provenance: Julian, Hymn to King Helios; Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The foundational dyad of the ineffable source and its first, self-aware intellectual reflection.
Intra-tradition: The absolute limit of darkness and non-being set against the infinite superabundance of light.
Intra-tradition: The ritual 'god-work' depends entirely on the physical manipulation of these embedded divine tokens.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Historical transmission—Neoplatonic emanationism profoundly shaped medieval Kabbalistic conceptions of the infinite, hidden, and attributeless God.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Plotinus and the Gnostics shared the same Alexandrian milieu, both envisioning a transcendent, utterly alien singularity preceding all creation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, non-dual source of reality from which all phenomenal existence or illusion springs, fundamentally beyond predication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unnameable, transcendent source that effortlessly generates the manifold world without willful action or depletion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the divine fullness—the collective, eternal realm of spiritual realities and archetypes standing immediately below the absolute source.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Hermetic philosophy explicitly conflated the Egyptian god of wisdom with the Divine Intellect (Nous), viewing him as the mind that ordered the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The feminine divine principle that descends from the fullness to interact with matter; Plotinus directly attacked the Gnostic interpretation of her as 'fallen'.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Neoplatonism vehemently denies Manichaeism's premise. For Plotinus, darkness is completely devoid of agency or being; it is merely the exhausted limit of light, not an opposing rival.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ritual practitioner forcefully bypasses ordinary consciousness, traveling through unseen metaphysical hierarchies to negotiate directly with cosmic forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The use of rhythmic, sensory, and intense ritual extremities to shatter ordinary perception and physically channel divine energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specific physical materials—minerals, herbs, animal parts—that are ritually awakened as active vessels for divine or spiritual resonance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conviction that the material world is encoded with a readable network of divine signatures, sympathies, and cosmic maps.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The innate, gravitational necessity of the purified soul to travel upward, shedding material weight to rest permanently in the highest echelon of reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Late antiquity extensively merged these cosmic savior-goddesses as the supreme, luminous mediators between heaven and the material world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, cosmic mother figure who bridges the unapproachable, absolute creator and the active, generated universe.

GOLD SEAM: Documented transmission — Pseudo-Dionysius (c. 500) carried Proclus's ascent-and-return metaphysics into Christian mysticism (shown by Koch and Stiglmayr, 1895), and through him theosis speaks emanationist grammar baptized.
GOLD SEAM: Both hold that matter can carry a charge of divine presence upward — though icon theology insists the image mediates to a prototype rather than housing a resident power.

GOLD SEAM: Both are late-antique-descended ritual technologies for union with the divine that recruit the body — breath, posture, repeated formulae — against the claim that intellect alone can ascend.
GOLD SEAM: The Epistles pour Plotinian emanation into Islamic form via the Arabic Plotinus corpus (the so-called Theology of Aristotle) — documented textual dependence, not mere resemblance.
Late-antique Neoplatonists built their cosmic Hekate Soteira on this Hymn's torch-bearing mediatrix, transposing the local cult-figure into a metaphysical world-soul principle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both are the first intelligible emanation proceeding from an unknowable absolute, the hinge between the ineffable One and a plural cosmos.

Iamblichus's 'De Mysteriis' directly engages and defends the Hermetic telestikē as a species of the same theurgic art Neoplatonism systematizes — a documented cross-school exchange over statue-animation.

His tanzih doctrine (God beyond being and non-being) directly adapts the Plotinian One via the Arabic Plotinus corpus (the Theology of Aristotle) — the same textual channel already documented for the Rasa'il Ikhwan al-Safa.
Both posit a single undifferentiated absolute from which a graded hierarchy of intelligences (Logoi / Nous, Psyche) emanates without diminishing the source.
Plotinus's Enneads VI.9 is the founding articulation of the One as source of the entire emanative scheme.
Editor, biographer, and arranger of Plotinus's treatises into the six Enneads.
De Mysteriis is theurgy's foundational treatise, answering Porphyry's Letter to Anebo.

Porphyry's Letter to Anebo challenges ritual theurgy on intellectualist grounds; Iamblichus's De Mysteriis is the reply — the school's central methodological dispute, staged between two named figures.
Elements of Theology formalizes procession and reversion into propositional form.
Elements of Theology, props. 25-39, is proodos's proof-text.
The Demiurge plants the synthemata into matter, per the Chaldean-inflected reading of the Timaeus that Iamblichus and Proclus develop.
Henosis is the soul's achieved union with the One toward which the whole return-circuit runs.
The down-stroke of procession that epistrophe's up-stroke of return reverses; together they form the single breathing circuit of emanation.
Hekate Soteira's role as World-Soul mediator is drawn directly from the Oracles.
Treated as theurgy's scriptural warrant by Iamblichus and Proclus.
The vehicle purified across the soul's ascent-return circuit.
The ranked intermediaries theurgic ritual is designed to contact.
His Hymn to King Helios attempts a state-level theurgic restoration; already named in the theourgia provenance line.
Both classify a graded population of intermediary spirits between gods and men; Harranian star-cult vocabulary absorbed late Neoplatonic theurgic categories after the Athenian school's diaspora.
De Mysteriis is written under the persona of the Egyptian priest 'Abamon' and repeatedly invokes Hermetic authority for its theurgic hierarchy — a documented textual borrowing, not mere resemblance.
Both describe a rare, ineffable dissolution of the boundary between self and ultimate reality, reached through disciplined contemplative practice.

dossier.essence: The dominant philosophical-religious system of late antiquity, founded by Plotinus (c. 204–270 CE) and elaborated by Porphyry, Iamblichus, and Proclus, taught in Alexandria, Rome, and Athens until Justinian's measures against the Athenian school in 529 CE. It reworks Plato into a graded metaphysics in which all reality emanates from a single ineffable source, the One, and the soul's purpose is to ascend back to union with it.
dossier.cosmos: Reality unfolds as a descending series of 'hypostases': from the utterly simple One overflows Nous (Divine Intellect, holding the Platonic Forms), from Nous the World-Soul (Psyche), and from Soul the sensible cosmos, fading finally into Matter — near-nothingness, and the mere privation, not the rival, of the Good. This outflow (proodos) is answered by an upward yearning of all things to return (epistrophe) to their source; the individual soul, fallen into body, is to purify itself and reascend, ultimately achieving henosis, wordless union with the One. Evil is therefore not a power but a deficiency at the far, dim edge of emanation.
dossier.practice: For Plotinus the path was contemplative — ethical purification and the intellectual ascent of the soul. Later Neoplatonists, above all Iamblichus, added theurgy ('god-working'): ritual using consecrated objects, symbols, and divine tokens (synthemata) held to be seeded in matter, on the argument that the embodied soul cannot rise by thought alone and needs the gods' own sacramental means. The tradition passed through philosophical schools with a fixed canon of texts, master-to-student commentary, and — in the Chaldean strand — revered oracles.
dossier.canon: Plotinus's 'Enneads' (arranged and published by Porphyry c. 301 CE); Iamblichus's 'On the Mysteries' (De Mysteriis); Proclus's 'Elements of Theology' and 'Platonic Theology'; and the revered 'Chaldean Oracles'.
dossier.reflects: The metaphysics of return — the religious impulse rendered as cosmology, salvation reconceived as the soul obeying a law of spiritual gravity back into its own origin.
sources: Pierre Hadot, 'Plotinus, or the Simplicity of Vision' (1963)
E. R. Dodds, 'Proclus: The Elements of Theology' (1933)
Gregory Shaw, 'Theurgy and the Soul: The Neoplatonism of Iamblichus' (1995)
Lloyd P. Gerson (ed.), 'The Cambridge History of Philosophy in Late Antiquity' (2010)
